subject: Mathematics
solution:
Class midpoints: $6, 10, 14, 18$.
Frequencies: $3, \lambda, 4, 7$. $N = 14 + \lambda$.
Mean $\mu = \frac{200 + 10\lambda}{14 + \lambda}$.
$\sum f_i x_i^2 = 3(36) + 100\lambda + 4(196) + 7(324) = 3160 + 100\lambda$.
Variance: $19 = \frac{3160+100\lambda}{N} - \mu^2$.
Let $u = 14+\lambda$:
$19u^2 = u(1760+100u) - (60+10u)^2 \Rightarrow 19u^2 - 560u + 3600 = 0$.
$u = \frac{560 \pm 200}{38}$, so $u = 20 \Rightarrow \lambda = 6$.
$\mu = \frac{260}{20} = 13$. Hence $\lambda + \mu = 6 + 13 = 19$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given $\sum_{i=1}^{10}(x_i+2)^2=180$

$\Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i + 40 = 180$

$\Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 + 4\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 140$

Also given $\sum_{i=1}^{10}(x_i-1)^2=90$

$\Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i + 10 = 90$

$\Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 80$

Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$6\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 60 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = 10$

Substituting this value in the second equation:

$\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 - 2(10) = 80 \Rightarrow \sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i^2 = 100$

The standard deviation is given by:

$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum x_i^2}{n} - \left(\dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}\right)^2}$

$\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{100}{10} - \left(\dfrac{10}{10}\right)^2}$

$\sigma = \sqrt{10 - 1} = \sqrt{9} = 3$

Answer: $3$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$a, b, c \in N \quad a < b < c$
$\overline{\mathrm{x}}=$ mean $=\frac{9+25+\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c}}{5}=18$
$a+b+c=56$
Mean deviation $=\frac{\Sigma\left|\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right|}{\mathrm{n}}=4$
$\begin{aligned} & =9+7+|18-\mathrm{a}|+|18-\mathrm{b}|+|18-\mathrm{c}|=20 \\ & =|18-\mathrm{a}|+|18-\mathrm{b}|+|18-\mathrm{c}|=4\end{aligned}$
Variance $=\frac{\Sigma\left|\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}-\overline{\mathrm{x}}\right|^2}{\mathrm{n}}=\frac{136}{5}$
$\begin{aligned} & =81+49+|18-a|^2+|18-b|^2+|18-c|^2=136 \\ & =(18-a)^2+(18-b)^2+(18-c)^2=6\end{aligned}$
Possible values $(18-\mathrm{a})^2=1, \quad(18-\mathrm{b})^2=1 \quad(18-\mathrm{c})^2=4$ $\mathrm{a} < \mathrm{b} < \mathrm{c}$
so $\quad \begin{array}{ccc}18-a=1 & 18-b=-1 & 18-c=-2 \\ \mathrm{a}=17 & \mathrm{~b}=19 & \mathrm{c}=20\end{array}$
$a+b+c=56$
$2 \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c} 34=19-20=33$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The sequence $k, 2k, 3k, \ldots, 1000k$ has 1000 terms.
The median is $\frac{500k + 501k}{2} = \frac{1001k}{2}$.
The mean deviation is $MD = \frac{1}{1000}\sum_{i=1}^{1000}|ik - \text{median}|$.
For $i \leq 500$: deviation = $\frac{1001k}{2} - ik$, giving $\sum_{i=1}^{500}(\frac{1001}{2} - i) = 125000$.
By symmetry, the sum for $i \geq 501$ is also $125000$.
Thus $MD = \frac{k}{1000} \cdot 250000 = 250k = 500$, so $k = 2$ and $k^2 = 4$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Mean } \overline{\mathrm{x}}=5.5 \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{10} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}=5.5 \times 10=55 \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{10} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^2=371 \\ & \left(\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}\right)_{\text {new }}=55-(4+5)+(6+8)=60 \\ & \\ & \left(\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^2\right)_{\text {new }}=371-\left(4^2+5^2\right)+\left(6^2+8^2\right)=430\end{aligned}$


$\begin{aligned} & \text { Variance } \sigma^2=\frac{\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}^2}{10}-\left(\frac{\sum \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{i}}}{10}\right)^2 \\ & \sigma^2=\frac{430}{10}-\left(\frac{60}{10}\right)^2 \\ & \sigma^2=43-36 \\ & \sigma^2=7\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
The given variable $X$ takes values $x_r = r(r-1)$ with frequencies $f_r = {}^{n}C_{r}$ for $r = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$.

The mean of the data is given by $\bar{X} = \dfrac{\sum_{r=0}^{n} f_r x_r}{\sum_{r=0}^{n} f_r}$.

We know that $\sum_{r=0}^{n} f_r = \sum_{r=0}^{n} {}^{n}C_{r} = 2^n$.

The sum of the products is:

$\sum_{r=0}^{n} f_r x_r = \sum_{r=0}^{n} r(r-1)\, {}^{n}C_{r}$

Using the property $r(r-1)\, {}^{n}C_{r} = n(n-1)\, {}^{n-2}C_{r-2}$, we get:

$\sum_{r=2}^{n} n(n-1)\, {}^{n-2}C_{r-2} = n(n-1)\, 2^{n-2}$

Substituting these into the mean formula:

$\bar{X} = \dfrac{n(n-1)\, 2^{n-2}}{2^n} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{4}$

Given that the mean is $60$:

$\dfrac{n(n-1)}{4} = 60 \Rightarrow n(n-1) = 240$

Since $16 \times 15 = 240$, we have $n = 16$.

The total frequency is $N = 2^{16}$. Since $N$ is even, the median is the average of the $(2^{15})$-th and $(2^{15} + 1)$-th observations.

The cumulative frequency up to $r = 7$ is:

$\sum_{r=0}^{7} {}^{16}C_{r} = \dfrac{2^{16} - {}^{16}C_{8}}{2} = 2^{15} - \dfrac{1}{2}\, {}^{16}C_{8}$

This value is strictly less than $2^{15}$.

The cumulative frequency up to $r = 8$ is:

$\sum_{r=0}^{8} {}^{16}C_{r} = 2^{15} + \dfrac{1}{2}\, {}^{16}C_{8}$

This value is strictly greater than $2^{15}$.

Thus, both the $(2^{15})$-th and $(2^{15} + 1)$-th observations fall in the class corresponding to $r = 8$.

The value of the variable for $r = 8$ is $x_8 = 8(8-1) = 56$.

Therefore, the median is $56$.

Answer: $56$
